evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (line ~280) and exploreMultiSeed (line ~300) are pure one-line pass-throughs to CollocateProfileComparator — no added behaviour, policy, or translation at either delegation site

SemanticFieldExplorer stores ownsExecutor=false in the only constructor (line 86); the conditional in close() (line 330) can never be true — dead abstraction for a lifecycle pattern that was never instantiated
WordSketchApiServer stores executor and indexPath as instance fields (lines 35-37, 45-46) but neither is read after the constructor delegates them — stored fields that serve no runtime purpose

QueryExecutor and PosTagger each have exactly one implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor, SimpleTagger); PosTagger is defensible (docstring explicitly names a future UDPipe-backed impl); QueryExecutor serves as a genuine adapter seam isolating BlackLab API volatility
RelationConfig record carries behaviour methods (getFullPattern, collocateReversePattern, collocatePosGroup, getDeprel) — data and related logic co-located cleanly, no leakage into handlers
CqlUtils contains exactly two utilities (escapeForRegex, splitCqlTokens) used by both GrammarConfigLoader and PatternSubstitution — focused, not a dumping ground
buildExploreResponseBody() in ExplorationHandlers eliminates response-assembly duplication across single-seed and multi-seed handlers — abstraction that earns its keep

Phase decomposition in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounProfiles, scoreNouns, identifyCoreCollocates) reduces the main method to readable orchestration without creating new public surface
AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, Edge, and DiscoveredNoun all use public final fields with direct access (e.g. dn.noun, dn.sharedCount, adj.presentInCount, adj.maxLogDice)
ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult use private final fields with explicit getters (getSeed(), getCoreCollocates(), getNouns(), getAllAdjectives())
QueryResults contains a mix: WordSketchResult uses private final + getters, while SnippetResult/KwicResult/CollocateResult are Java records with accessor methods
ExploreOptions (model package) is a Java record; ExploreParams is a private local record inside ExplorationHandlers used as an intermediate parse result — partially overlapping with ExploreOptions
All non-model code is consistently camelCase; package layout is sensible and stable; handler classes follow a uniform package-private + constructor-injection pattern
TaggedToken (tagging package) uses private final fields with getters, consistent with the private-field camp but distinct from model peers like DiscoveredNoun
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: The model package has three coexisting access strategies for domain objects (public fields, private+getters, records) without documented rationale. This forces a developer to check each class individually before reading or writing against it, adding unnecessary friction.

evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore assembles domain objects (bcqlPattern via getFullPattern, reverseCollocatePattern via collocateReversePattern, ExploreOptions with headPos/collocatePos) inside the HTTP handler; a TODO comment explicitly acknowledges this: 'domain object assembly should be delegated to a factory/service rather than assembled in the HTTP handler'

Dead null guard at ExplorationHandlers.java:56 — 'if (grammarConfig == null)' can never be true because the constructor already throws IllegalArgumentException for null grammarConfig; same residual guard recurs at line 142 inside handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti

Unsafe unchecked cast in single-seed handler (line 109): '((Map<String, Object>) response.get("parameters")).put("nouns_per", nounsPerCollocate)' — the handler reaches into the nested map returned by buildBaseExploreResponse to inject a single extra key, coupling the handler tightly to the internal structure of the builder output

Multi-seed handler (lines 178-182) assembles a 'common_collocates' list from result.getCoreCollocates() as a flat word list and adds it to the response, then calls buildExploreResponseBody which also iterates result.getCoreCollocates() to build the richer 'core_collocates' list — both lists are derived from the same data source, creating structural redundancy between them

RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint (a private method on a record) emits logger.warn for deprecated tag= attributes — a pure classification method on a value type is mixing in observable side effects, making the method impure without documenting this in its signature

executeSurfacePattern and findCollocations in BlackLabQueryExecutor both: parse a BCQL pattern, execute it via searchHits/HitGroups, extract a collocate lemma per group, and call buildAndRankCollocates — the distinction between them (labeled vs unlabeled positions, group strategy) is real but the structural repetition is high; a shared extraction helper could reduce the parallel logic

handleConcordanceExamples in SketchHandlers.java (lines ~195-230) also assembles bcqlQuery directly from grammarConfig.getRelation(...) and PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate inside the HTTP handler, matching the same pattern-assembly-in-handler concern seen in ExplorationHandlers

QueryExecutor interface is documented with clear Javadoc distinguishing findCollocations/executeQuery/executeBcqlQuery/executeSurfacePattern/executeDependencyPattern — each method's contract and parser used is spelled out
HttpApiUtils provides a uniform set of helpers (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, requireParam, parseQueryParams) used consistently by both SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers
ExploreOptions record replaces what was a 10-argument method signature: ExplorationHandlers.java line 91 shows the clean 7-field record construction

Full-sketch response shape (handleFullSketchImpl): {lemma, relations: {id: {name, cql, collocations:[...]}}} differs from single-relation shape (handleRelationQueryInternal): {lemma, relation, collocations:[...]} — siblings in the same /api/sketch/{lemma} family but incompatible schemas

ExploreOptions still carries headPos/collocatePos fields (ExploreOptions.java lines 13-14), set in ExplorationHandlers line 88-92, but SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern never reads opts.headPos() or opts.collocatePos() — dead fields left over after executeSurfacePattern interface cleanup
handleSemanticField (ExplorationHandlers line 230) puts 'nouns' in response while accepting 'seeds' as input parameter — input/output key name divergence in the same endpoint

handleConcordanceExamples (SketchHandlers) sets raw: r.rawXml() != null ? r.rawXml() : r.sentence() — falls back to sentence text when rawXml is null, while handleBcqlQueryPost defaults raw to empty string for the same null case

executeDependencyPattern is exposed in QueryExecutor interface and called only from Main.java CLI path (line 216); HTTP sketch handlers call executeSurfacePattern for both SURFACE and DEP relations, making executeDependencyPattern invisible to the REST API

handleSemanticFieldExplore manually extracts bcqlPattern/reverseCollocatePattern/headPos/collocatePos from RelationConfig and constructs ExploreOptions before calling exploreByPattern — annotated with a TODO; handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti passes RelationConfig directly to exploreMultiSeed, a more direct delegation

strengths: RelationConfig record bundles pattern-parsing behaviour (getFullPattern, collocateReversePattern, collocatePosGroup) with its data, preventing callers from leaking POS regex logic into HTTP handlers
buildExploreResponseBody() and buildBaseExploreResponse() in ExplorationHandlers are genuine deduplication extractions called from two sibling handlers
CqlUtils is a tight, focused utility class with exactly two responsibilities used across two callers — not a god module
Phase decomposition of SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern into four private helpers improves readability without inflating the public API surface
HttpApiUtils collects uniform HTTP response concerns (CORS headers, JSON serialization, error format) in one place with no logic leakage to callers
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The issues are concentrated in a single class (SemanticFieldExplorer): two methods are pure delegation pass-throughs to CollocateProfileComparator with no added value, and one dead boolean field (ownsExecutor) betrays an abstraction that was designed but never instantiated. These are isolated symptoms of a refactor that stopped slightly short of completion rather than a systemic indirection problem.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly in the 80 range: most abstractions clearly earn their keep (RelationConfig, CqlUtils, phase decomposition, response builders) and the overall architecture avoids pass-through wrapper chains. The score is pulled down by two pure delegation methods in SemanticFieldExplorer that add a call hop callers must trace through without benefit, plus dead lifecycle machinery (ownsExecutor) and unused stored fields in WordSketchApiServer — enough recurring friction to land at 80 rather than 88-90, but not pervasive enough to fall to 70.

strengths: All Java identifiers use standard camelCase consistently throughout the entire codebase — no snake_case drift anywhere
Package layout cleanly separates concerns: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, tagging, utils, viz — no orphaned files or mis-placed types
Handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) follow an identical structural pattern: package-private, constructor-injected, no static methods on the handler path
Utility classes (HttpApiUtils, CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator) consistently use the final-class-with-private-constructor idiom
Logger declarations are uniform across all classes: private static final Logger logger = LoggerFactory.getLogger(X.class)
convention_outlier.issue_character: The convention drift is localized to the model package, where three distinct access patterns (public fields, private+getters, records) coexist across sibling domain types without a documented rationale, creating a style island inside an otherwise uniform codebase.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: Naming and structural conventions are exemplary across the whole codebase — a developer can navigate any package and immediately understand the layout. The only genuine outlier is the model layer, where AdjectiveProfile/CoreCollocate/Edge/DiscoveredNoun expose public fields while ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult use getters and QueryResults subtypes are records. This split is noticeable and forces callers to context-switch between access styles (dn.sharedCount vs result.getSeed()). It is a real but contained issue, pulling the score from an 85+ down to the low 70s — solid but uneven.

strengths: buildExploreResponseBody and buildBaseExploreResponse correctly centralize shared JSON response assembly used by both explore handlers, eliminating previous duplication
ExploreParams record neatly groups the three common numeric HTTP parameters, giving them a typed home and a single parse path
Handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) meaningfully separate HTTP routing concerns from service and executor layers
CollocateProfileComparator was cleanly extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to enforce size discipline and single responsibility
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() correctly moves the POS regex derivation out of the HTTP handler into the config record where it belongs
design_coherence.issue_character: HTTP handlers are still assembling domain objects (patterns, options, parameter structs) that belong in a service or factory layer, and a few structural residuals — dead null guards, an unsafe nested-map cast, and side effects in a pure record method — indicate the refactoring was partially completed rather than carried through.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase scores above the failing zone (40-55) because it has genuine structural decomposition: handler classes, extracted comparator, centralized response builders, and config-owned POS logic. However, it falls short of the 75-80 range because handler methods still own domain assembly (acknowledged via TODO), dead guards persist after constructor hardening, and a record method emits side effects. These are design coherence problems, not just style issues — they reflect responsibility boundaries that have been identified but not yet enforced.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface uses clear Javadoc to distinguish four similar-looking methods (findCollocations vs executeBcqlQuery vs executeSurfacePattern vs executeDependencyPattern), making method selection unambiguous for implementors
HttpApiUtils is a single, focused utility used identically by all handler classes — no ad-hoc JSON construction or HTTP response code scattered elsewhere
ExploreOptions record consolidates what was previously spread across multiple positional arguments, improving call-site readability
BCQL POST response fields (raw, collocate_lemma, frequency, log_dice) are always present with defined defaults, so consumers never need to null-check these fields
resolveRelationAlias() in ExplorationHandlers centralises all relation-name aliasing in a single switch — both explore endpoints share the same normalisation logic
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Issues are scattered individually across several boundaries rather than forming a single deep structural defect: dead fields in ExploreOptions, a response-key naming mismatch, divergent response shapes for sketch siblings, and an inconsistent null-fallback strategy for 'raw' — each isolated, but collectively they indicate that cleanup passes have been applied unevenly across the API surface.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase is solidly above the 60 threshold for 'significant drag': the core interfaces are well-documented, utilities are used consistently, and recent cleanup (relations key unification, seeds validation, null-defaulting BCQL fields) shows deliberate API discipline. What pulls the score below 80 is a set of real developer-friction points: the full-sketch vs single-relation response shape divergence will surprise a client developer, the 'nouns'/'seeds' input-output mismatch in /api/semantic-field is a subtle contract violation, and ExploreOptions carrying dead headPos/collocatePos fields creates confusion about what the explorer actually uses. These are not catastrophic but a developer integrating the API will encounter each of them, placing the score at 70.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles and exploreMultiSeed are pure one-liner delegations to CollocateProfileComparator with no added value
issues.suggestion: Either let ExplorationHandlers depend directly on CollocateProfileComparator for compareCollocateProfiles/exploreMultiSeed (eliminating the pass-through), or merge CollocateProfileComparator back into SemanticFieldExplorer as private methods since the public surface adds no value. If SemanticFieldExplorer must remain the single dependency of ExplorationHandlers, add a brief doc comment explaining why the delegation is intentional (e.g. lifecycle or future policy hook).
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles body: `return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, minLogDice, maxPerNoun);` — identical signature, zero logic added
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed body: `return comparator.exploreMultiSeed(seeds, relationConfig, minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared);` — identical signature, zero logic added
ExplorationHandlers holds a SemanticFieldExplorer and calls both methods; it could depend on CollocateProfileComparator directly for these two operations, removing the empty intermediary hop

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.ownsExecutor is always false — the lifecycle abstraction it guards can never activate
issues.suggestion: Remove the ownsExecutor field and the dead branch in close(). If executor lifecycle ownership is needed in future, re-introduce it as a second constructor parameter or factory method at that time rather than carrying a permanently-false flag.
evidence: Only one constructor exists (line 84): `this.ownsExecutor = false;` — no code path ever sets it to true
close() (line 330) contains `if (ownsExecutor && executor instanceof AutoCloseable ac)` — this branch is unreachable dead code
The field and the conditional suggest an ownership-transfer constructor was planned but never implemented

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer stores executor and indexPath as instance fields but neither is read after constructor delegation
issues.suggestion: Remove both fields. Use local variables in the constructor when passing executor and indexPath to SketchHandlers and SemanticFieldExplorer, since no post-construction access occurs.
evidence: Fields declared at lines 35-37: `private final QueryExecutor executor` and `private final String indexPath`
Both are assigned in the constructor (lines 45-46) and then only passed into SketchHandlers/SemanticFieldExplorer — never referenced again in any method
A developer scanning the class would assume these fields are used for something, then spend time searching for their usage

issues.summary: Three coexisting field-access strategies in the model package: public fields, private+getters, and records
issues.suggestion: Converge all model value types to Java records. Convert AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, Edge, and DiscoveredNoun to records; their constructors map 1:1 to record components. This aligns them with ExploreOptions and the QueryResults subtypes, eliminates the public mutable surface (the fields are final but still public), and unifies access syntax. ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult have richer behaviour (filtering/edge-building methods) and could remain as classes with private fields + getters, or become records with additional methods — document the rationale either way.
evidence: AdjectiveProfile declares `public final String adjective`, `public final int presentInCount`, `public final double avgLogDice`, etc. — all accessed directly as dn.adjective, adj.presentInCount across the codebase
CoreCollocate, Edge, DiscoveredNoun follow the same public-field pattern
ExplorationResult declares all fields private with explicit getters: getSeed(), getSeedCollocates(), getDiscoveredNouns(), getCoreCollocates()
ComparisonResult similarly uses private fields + getters: getNouns(), getAllAdjectives()
QueryResults.SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult are Java records; ExploreOptions is a Java record — accessor syntax differs again
Callers switch between styles in the same method body, e.g. ExplorationHandlers line 307 uses dn.noun (public field) and line 107 uses result.getSeed() (getter)

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExplore assembles domain objects (bcqlPattern, reverseCollocatePattern, ExploreOptions) inside the HTTP handler instead of delegating to a service/factory
issues.suggestion: Introduce a method or factory on SemanticFieldExplorer (or a new ExploreRequest builder) that accepts a RelationConfig and seed string and returns the assembled ExploreOptions + patterns, so the handler only parses HTTP params and delegates.
evidence: Lines 87-100: bcqlPattern = relationConfig.get().getFullPattern(seed); reverseCollocatePattern = ...; headPos = ...; ExploreOptions opts = new ExploreOptions(...) all assembled in-handler
An explicit TODO at line 84 acknowledges this: 'domain object assembly should be delegated to a factory/service rather than assembled in the HTTP handler'

issues.summary: Dead null checks for grammarConfig inside handler methods that can never be reached after constructor hardening
issues.suggestion: Remove both dead null guards from the handler methods; the constructor precondition is the correct enforcement point.
evidence: Constructor (line ~33) throws IllegalArgumentException for null grammarConfig, making the field non-null for all reachable instances
handleSemanticFieldExplore (line 56) and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti (line 142) both still contain 'if (grammarConfig == null) { sendError(500, ...) }' branches that are unreachable dead code

issues.summary: Single-seed handler reaches into builder output via unchecked cast to inject one extra key into the nested parameters map
issues.suggestion: Add a nouns_per parameter to buildBaseExploreResponse (or a variant), or return a typed response builder object so the handler can set endpoint-specific parameters through a typed API rather than casting and mutating the nested map directly.
evidence: Line 109: ((Map<String, Object>) response.get("parameters")).put("nouns_per", nounsPerCollocate) — unchecked cast couples handler to buildBaseExploreResponse's internal structure
buildBaseExploreResponse returns a mutable HashMap, so this works at runtime, but the caller must know the internal key and type of the nested map

issues.summary: Multi-seed handler iterates result.getCoreCollocates() twice: once for common_collocates (flat word list) and once inside buildExploreResponseBody for core_collocates (rich objects)
issues.suggestion: Derive common_collocates from core_collocates inside buildExploreResponseBody (or post-process the already-built core_collocates list), eliminating the pre-pass in the handler.
evidence: Lines 178-182: handler iterates getCoreCollocates() to build a flat 'common_collocates' list and adds it to response
Line 356 (inside buildExploreResponseBody): the same getCoreCollocates() is iterated again to build the richer 'core_collocates' list — two response keys derived from one source in separate passes

issues.summary: RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint emits logger.warn from a pure classification method on a record, mixing observable side effects into a value-type helper
issues.suggestion: Move the deprecation warning to GrammarConfigLoader at load time (where the pattern string is first parsed and the tag= attribute would be detected), keeping posGroupFromConstraint pure. Alternatively, emit the warning once at construction rather than on every call.
evidence: Lines 166-173: six branches for deprecated tag= attribute each call logger.warn(...) before returning the PosGroup — a pure classifier is emitting log side effects
Records are idiomatic Java value types; their accessor logic should be referentially transparent; callers cannot know this method logs without reading its implementation

issues.summary: Full-sketch and single-relation sketch endpoints return structurally incompatible response shapes
issues.suggestion: Have the single-relation endpoint wrap its collocations inside the same per-relation object shape as the full endpoint: {lemma, relations: {<id>: {name, cql, collocations:[...]}}}. This makes the single-relation response a predictable subset of the full-sketch response.
evidence: handleFullSketchImpl returns {lemma, relations: {id: {name, cql, collocations:[...]}}} — collocations nested two levels deep under a relation-keyed map
handleRelationQueryInternal returns {lemma, relation, collocations:[...]} — collocations flat at the top level, no per-relation metadata
Both are served under /api/sketch/{lemma} and /api/sketch/{lemma}/{relation} — a client switching between the two must handle two incompatible schemas

issues.summary: ExploreOptions.headPos and collocatePos are set by handler but never read by SemanticFieldExplorer
issues.suggestion: Remove headPos and collocatePos from ExploreOptions. If position hints are ever needed again, pass them through RelationConfig directly or derive them from the pattern inside the executor.
evidence: ExploreOptions record (lines 13-14) declares headPos and collocatePos fields
ExplorationHandlers lines 88-92 reads headPosition/collocatePosition from RelationConfig and passes them into ExploreOptions
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern unpacks only opts.topCollocates(), opts.nounsPerCollocate(), opts.minLogDice(), opts.minShared() — never touches opts.headPos() or opts.collocatePos()
executeSurfacePattern in QueryExecutor no longer accepts these position hints after the interface cleanup, confirming the fields are now orphaned

issues.summary: /api/semantic-field accepts 'seeds' parameter but puts 'nouns' key in the response body
issues.suggestion: Change line 230 from response.put("nouns", ...) to response.put("seeds", ...) to match the input parameter name and align with the multi-seed endpoint convention.
evidence: handleSemanticField line ~194: String nounsParam = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(exchange, params, "seeds") — input key is 'seeds'
handleSemanticField line 230: response.put("nouns", new ArrayList<>(result.getNouns())) — response key is 'nouns'
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti line 174 uses response.put("seeds", ...) consistently — making the /api/semantic-field endpoint the only outlier

issues.summary: concordance/examples 'raw' field falls back to sentence text on null, while bcql endpoint defaults to empty string
issues.suggestion: Align handleConcordanceExamples to use the same fallback as handleBcqlQueryPost: r.rawXml() != null ? r.rawXml() : "". If the sentence text is also needed, expose it via a separate 'sentence' field (which is already present).
evidence: handleConcordanceExamples: exMap.put("raw", r.rawXml() != null ? r.rawXml() : r.sentence()) — raw becomes the sentence string when rawXml is null
handleBcqlQueryPost: resultMap.put("raw", r.rawXml() != null ? r.rawXml() : "") — raw becomes empty string when rawXml is null
Both endpoints return CollocateResult objects and expose a 'raw' field, so consumers expect the same null contract

issues.summary: Single-seed explore handler assembles domain config manually while multi-seed passes RelationConfig directly to the service
issues.suggestion: Add an overload or refactor exploreByPattern to accept RelationConfig directly (mirroring exploreMultiSeed), and move bcqlPattern/reversePattern/position extraction into the service layer. This removes the domain-assembly responsibility from the HTTP handler.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore (lines ~82-95): extracts bcqlPattern, reverseCollocatePattern, headPos, collocatePos from RelationConfig, constructs ExploreOptions, then calls semanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(seed, name, bcql, reversePattern, opts) — 5 arguments extracted from config
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: calls semanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed(seeds, relationConfig.get(), minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared) — passes RelationConfig directly
The handler even has a TODO comment acknowledging that domain object assembly should be delegated to a factory/service
This means evolving RelationConfig requires coordinated changes to the handler (single-seed) but not the service (multi-seed)
